If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player moved UP 7 cells and stopped (noop_short_circuit). The player is now at the top of the narrow panel, near the R block.
Now let me try a new hypothesis: what if I need to extend the path RIGHT to touch the blocks in a specific order, and the path "collects" them? But earlier when I did this, the blocks didn't disappear.
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier I moved RIGHT from the top and the blocks appeared next to the player. But now the blocks are still on the main board. Maybe the blocks "reset" when the player moves away?
Let me try extending RIGHT from the top and see what happens now.
Actually, I've been going in circles. Let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to create a path that connects all 3 blocks in the order shown on the bottom panel (R, N, b)?
The bottom panel shows: M-R-N-b. This could mean the path should go: from player (M), to R, to N, to b.
Currently the blocks are arranged vertically: R (top), b (middle), N (bottom).
To visit them in order R, N, b, I would need to go: R (top) -> N (bottom) -> b (middle).
This means: extend RIGHT to touch R, then extend DOWN to touch N, then extend UP to touch b.
But the player can only move in the narrow panel on the left. The path extends RIGHT from the player's position.
Let me try: from the top position, extend RIGHT to touch R, then move DOWN to the N block's row, extend RIGHT to touch N, then move UP to the b block's row, extend RIGHT to touch b.
Actually wait, the path extends from the player's current position. If I move the player, the path moves with the player. So I can't leave a path behind.
Let me re-examine: when I extend RIGHT, the path extends from the player. When I move the player, the path moves with the player. So the path is always attached to the player.
This means I can only touch one block at a time (the one at the player's current row).
What if the goal is to touch the blocks in the order R, N, b? I would:
1. Start at top (player is there now)
2. Extend RIGHT to touch R
3. Move DOWN to N's row (2 rows down)
4. Extend RIGHT to touch N
5. Move UP to b's row (1 row up)
6. Extend RIGHT to touch b
Let me try this sequence.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try touching blocks in order R, N, b (matching bottom panel order)
# Player is at top, R block is at row 19-22
# First extend RIGHT to touch R
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
